title: CF 1305C – Kuroni and Impossible Calculation
content:
The problem statement can be found here.

Constraints

  • $2\le N \le 2\cdot 10^5$
  • $1\le M \le 10^3$
  • $0\le A_i\le 10^9$

Approach

At one glance this problem looks impossible to solve. The naive approach is to compute $|A_i-A_j| \mod M$ for all possible indices $i,j$ but this costs $\mathcal O(N^2)$ time, which will give TLE. One interesting thing to note is that, if any two elements of the array are equal, then the answer is $0$. (Why?) Another thing to note is the constraint on $M$. Why is $M\le 10^3$ and not some popular values like $10^9+7$? Is this possibly hinting at $\mathcal O(M^2)$ solution? Since the result is evaluated modulo $M$, we can replace the array $A_i$ by $A_i \mod M$. We can have maximum of $M$ unique values modulo $M$, which effectively breaks the problem in 2 cases:

Case 1: $N>M$
Atleast two elements of the array must be equal (Why?), and hence the answer must be $0$.

Case 2: $N\le M$
We can just find the answer by brute-force in $\mathcal O(M^2)$.

Thus the seemingly impossible problem has been solved in $\mathcal O(M^2)$ time.
